2d9abd31.narrative: Our marketplace is suffering a complete customer-engagement collapse, with zero new customers acquired, zero repeat purchases across all states, and a total absence of seller reviews over the past 12 months [2][5][1]. Despite this stagnation, credit cards continue to power nearly three-quarters of transactions, with installment values surging to $385 on high-value orders [3][8]. This stark disconnect—intact payment flow alongside frozen acquisition, retention, and seller feedback—signals a broken growth engine that demands immediate executive intervention.

citations.finding: Across all states, 0 customers fall into the 0–6 months (tenure_0_6_months) or 7–12 months (tenure_7_12_months) categories, indicating that no customers in the dataset made their first purchase within the 12 months leading up to April 9, 2020 — suggesting either data gaps in early purchase history or that all customers have longer tenures.

citations.insight_id: Customer__customer retention and repurchase behavior by state__11
citations.angle: repurchase behavior and retention potential by state
citations.finding: All states show 0% repeat customers (single-order customers only), indicating no customer made more than one purchase in the 12-month period ending April 9, 2020 — suggesting a lack of repurchase behavior across the entire customer base, regardless of state.

citations.angle: Review activity over the past 12 months, broken down by seller state, revealing a complete absence of reviews.
citations.finding: All 20 states (including NULL) show NULL average reviews per seller, indicating that across the entire seller base, no reviews were created in the last 12 months (2019-04-10 to 2020-04-10).

citations.angle: payment method distribution and installment behavior
citations.finding: Credit card is the dominant payment method (76,505 orders, 73.6% of total), with an average of 3.5 installments per order and an average value of $62.50 per installment; in contrast, boleto, voucher, and debit card are almost exclusively single-installment (1.0 average), but with much higher average values per installment ($145.03, $65.70, and $142.57 respectively), indicating installment usage is primarily a credit-card feature.

citations.angle: Distribution of multi-installment orders by order value quartile and payment method, with average payment value per installment
citations.finding: For orders with multiple installments, credit card is the only payment method used, with order counts rising steadily across quartiles (7,559 in Q1 to 17,535 in Q4), and average payment value per installment increasing sharply from $45.21 in the lowest-value quartile to $385.13 in the highest-value quartile—indicating that high-value orders not only use credit card installments more frequently but also involve larger per-installment amounts.

96f8857f.narrative: Marketing and Product have recorded zero experiments in the past year [1][6], while $1M+ in non-experiment spend generated virtually no measurable return [1] and all cross-functional KPIs suffer from definition gaps between Marketing and Finance [3] — leaving growth performance unmeasured and misaligned. Simultaneously, Procurement faces critical supplier delivery failures with multiple suppliers at 0% on-time performance [5], yet Operations reports no refunds tied to delayed suppliers [2] while Commerce reveals tens of thousands of refunds were processed without any captured monetary value [8], obscuring the true cost of fulfillment breakdowns. These parallel breakdowns in growth testing, metric definitions, and supply-chain tracking demand immediate intervention to restore visibility into revenue drivers and operational losses.
96f8857f.headline_theme: Critical Gaps in Growth and Operations

citations.angle: The absence of any A/B test experiment campaigns in the specified time window, and the resulting inability to assess ROI correlation with experimentation, represents a critical data or operational gap.
citations.finding: Non-experiment campaigns had an average spend of $1,001,307.89 and 44 total clicks, yielding an average ROI of 0.0 — meaning no measurable return (clicks per dollar) was achieved. The query returned only one row, indicating no campaigns were identified as A/B test experiments under the specified time window (start_ts ≥ 1747507200000 and end_ts ≤ 1779129600000), so a comparison with experiment campaigns is not possible.

citations.angle: The result reveals a critical gap: zero active experiments in the past year, suggesting either a complete pause in experimentation or a data pipeline issue—neither of which was previously known.
citations.finding: No active experiments were found in the last 12 months: the query returned 0 total experiments, with all aggregated metrics (average, min, max duration and traffic/variant ratios) as NULL, indicating no data met the filter criteria (start_ts >= 1747507200000 and end_ts >= 1747507200000).

citations.angle: The absence of any A/B test experiment campaigns in the specified time window, and the resulting inability to assess ROI correlation with experimentation, represents a critical data or operational gap.
citations.finding: Non-experiment campaigns had an average spend of $1,001,307.89 and 44 total clicks, yielding an average ROI of 0.0 — meaning no measurable return (clicks per dollar) was achieved. The query returned only one row, indicating no campaigns were identified as A/B test experiments under the specified time window (start_ts ≥ 1747507200000 and end_ts ≤ 1779129600000), so a comparison with experiment campaigns is not possible.

citations.angle: Semantic divergence categories across KPIs and cross-functional definition misalignment severity
citations.finding: All 3 KPIs fall into 'Other Divergence' categories, indicating that none of the predefined semantic divergence patterns (Aligned, User-Paying Customer, Revenue-Margin, or Gross-Recognized) fully capture the misalignment—each KPI exhibits unique, non-standardized definition mismatches between marketing and finance.

citations.angle: on-time delivery rate by supplier over the last 12 months
citations.finding: Among suppliers with at least 10 orders in the past 12 months, 10 suppliers achieved a 0% on-time delivery rate (e.g., SUP-001742, SUP-000135, SUP-001849), and the worst-performing supplier with any on-time deliveries (SUP-000934) had only 3.7% on-time delivery (1 out of 27 orders). The top performers among the lowest-rated group (e.g., SUP-000934, SUP-001293, SUP-000626) still had on-time rates below 11%.

citations.insight_id: Operations__supplier performance lifecycle: examine whether suppliers with repeated late deliveries (chronic delays) show different refund patterns than suppliers with occasional delays, controlling for volume and delay severity__11
citations.angle: Refund incidence by supplier delay classification, controlling for delay severity and volume
citations.finding: Among suppliers with ≥10 purchase orders in the last 12 months, chronically delayed suppliers (175 suppliers, ≥80% delayed orders) and occasionally delayed suppliers (1,825 suppliers, 20–79% delayed orders) both showed zero refunds—total, logistics-related, and customer-preference—despite differing delay rates (4.24 vs. 2.93 avg delay days) and order volumes (35 vs. 37.3 avg orders per supplier).

citations.angle: Refund value by payment method vs. total order value
citations.finding: The SQL query returned zero total refund value (USD) for all payment methods over the last 12 months, despite over 54,000 refunds per method (e.g., 54,587 for Google Pay), indicating refunds were recorded but their monetary value was not captured in the dataset — likely due to missing or unpopulated refund amount fields.
